answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to the elbow?
answers.intake_a3: no, but the pain started when I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: icing the first day and it helped a little with the pain
answers.intake_q7: Any pain when you move your wrist or grip objects?
answers.intake_q8: Any pain when you extend or straighten your elbow or when you lift your arm?
answers.intake_q9: Any pain when you bend your elbow or twist your forearm?
answers.intake_q10: Any difficulty or pain when you try to lift or carry objects with your arm?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain located on the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis, without any recent injury or trauma. She reports no swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow, and no numbness, tingling, or weakness in the arm or hand. She experiences pain when lifting objects but denies pain with wrist movement, elbow extension, bending, or twisting of the forearm. Initial treatment with icing provided some mild relief. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain associated with repetitive wrist extension activities such as tennis. The absence of swelling, neurological symptoms, or trauma supports this diagnosis.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure patient has no contraindications. 
- Return precautions: Advise patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, or if symptoms do not improve with conservative management over the next 2-4 weeks. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time; consider imaging or specialist referral if symptoms persist or worsen despite treatment.

Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…
